title: Dog Torn Dewclaw: Home Care and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn what to do for a dog's torn, bleeding, or hanging dewclaw. Understand urgency levels and how to provide first aid for your pet's nail injury.
content_html:
What is a Torn Dewclaw?

A torn dewclaw occurs when the nail located higher up on a dog's inner leg becomes snagged, cracked, or partially detached. Because the dewclaw is not worn down naturally by walking on pavement, it can grow long and easily hook onto carpet, brush, or furniture. When it tears, it often exposes the 'quick'—the sensitive living tissue containing blood vessels and nerves.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for a torn dewclaw is classified as Medium. While it is rarely a life-threatening situation, it is an incredibly painful injury for your dog. Because the nail is often still attached to the sensitive quick or even the underlying bone, every movement can cause sharp pain. You should contact a veterinarian for an appointment within 24 hours to have the damaged nail safely removed and to prevent a secondary infection.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear, close-up photo of the injury to your veterinary team can be extremely helpful for remote triage. A photo allows the vet to see exactly where the break occurred, whether the quick is fully exposed, and if there is active hemorrhaging. This visual information helps the clinic determine how quickly your dog needs to be seen and allows them to provide you with specific first-aid instructions tailored to the severity of the tear.

faqs.question: Should I try to pull the hanging nail off at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should not attempt to pull the nail off yourself. The dewclaw is attached to the bone, and pulling it can cause extreme pain and potential permanent damage. A veterinarian can remove it safely using local anesthesia or sedation.

faqs.question: How can I stop the bleeding immediately?
faqs.answer: Apply gentle pressure to the area with a clean cloth or gauze. If you have styptic powder, you can apply it to the tip of the nail. In a pinch, cornstarch or flour can also help encourage clotting.

faqs.question: Can I just wait for the nail to fall off on its own?
faqs.answer: Waiting is not recommended because an exposed quick is a direct pathway for bacteria to enter the body, which can lead to a serious infection of the nail bed or bone. Professional cleaning and clipping are necessary.
